---
title: "Synthesis"

execute:
  freeze: false
  cache: false

title-block-style: none

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: "ragg_png"
---

```{=latex}
\clearpage
\providecommand{\chaptermark}[1]{}
\ifcsname c@chapter\endcsname
\setcounter{chapter}{6}
\setcounter{section}{0}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{\protect\numberline{6}Synthesis}
\chaptermark{Synthesis}
\fi
\providecommand{\thechapter}{6}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
  \node[inner sep=0pt] at ([yshift=-3.5cm]current page.center)
    {\includegraphics[width=\paperwidth,height=\paperheight]{images/ch6_cover.jpg}};
  \fill[black]
    (current page.north west) rectangle ([yshift=-7cm]current page.north east);
  \node[color=thesissand, align=left,
        font=\fontsize{13}{16}\selectfont\sffamily, anchor=west]
    at ([xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-1.5cm]current page.north west)
    {Chapter \thechapter};
  \node[color=white, text width=0.85\paperwidth, align=left,
        font=\fontsize{22}{28}\selectfont\bfseries\sffamily, anchor=west]
    at ([xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-4cm]current page.north west)
    {Synthesis};
\end{tikzpicture}
\null
\clearpage
```

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
Chapter 6 Synthesis

![](images/ch6_cover.jpg){fig-align="center" width=15cm}

{{< pagebreak >}}
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
# Synthesis {.unnumbered}
:::

## Introduction
The chapters preceding this synthesis established the ecological and practical basis for using stone piles as a kōura habitat restoration tool. Behavioural experiments (Chapter 2) confirmed that kōura respond with heightened refuge use to novel predatores than native ones. Field observations (Chapter 3) identified the habitat features that support kōura along lake shorelines, and mesocosm trials (Chapter 4) showed that structure stability and complexity matter more than material when kōura select shelter. Chapter 5 demonstrated that stone piles deployed along the shoreline of Lake Rotoiti attracted kōura at densities approaching those on natural rocky substrates. This chapter will synthesise on the findings and main discussions of each chapter and presents two pilot studies that extend the work in different directions. The first trialled the construction logistics and kōura reintroduction in Lake Ōkaro. The second extended deployment into deeper water across a depth gradient and different monitoring methods will be discussed. Together with the main chapters, these case studies broaden the application of the restoration approach developed in this thesis for future deployment and monitoring.

## Three stressors working together
As documented by @Kusabs2026, the establishment of catfish (*Ameiurus nebulosus*) in Lakes Rotorua and Rotoiti has been accompanied by substantial declines in kōura abundance and biomass. Catfish predation is, however, only one of the three compounding stressors that together determine where kōura can persist in the Rotorua Te Arawa Lakes. The second stressor is thermal stratification and the seasonal anoxia it produces. As described in the general introduction, stratification compresses the zone of livable habitat for kōura into the shallow littoral margins of the lake, this is the same zone where catfish are most active. For fish, habitat compression is conventionally calculated volumetrically, since fish use the full water column. This volumetric loss is disproportionately large in shallow sloped lakes like Rotorua and Rotoiti compared to deep-basin lakes due to the concentration of water volume near the surface [@Detmer2026]. For kōura, as bottom-dwelling animals, the constraint is more severe as livable habitat is defined by bottom area, not volume, so compression of the oxygenated zone translates directly into a reduction of available benthic surface. Catfish are largely unaffected by this compression, tolerating both low oxygen and elevated temperatures, while kōura are driven upwards into the shallow zone where predation pressure is highest. Climate projections suggest this dynamic will intensify as stratification strengthens and anoxic events become more frequent and prolonged [@Jane2021; @Lee2025]. The third stressor is the presence of invasive macrophytes in the littoral zone. Dense macrophyte beds reduce the structural complexity of the shoreline, replacing and or covering up rocky habitats reducing preferred kōura habitat with a dense uniform layer that favours catfish. Macrophytes also contribute to oxygen depletion during die-off events, further reducing the habitable area available to kōura. The field surveys in Chapter 3 provide additional grounding for this compounding effect. Across the five monitored lakes, oligotrophic lakes with structurally complex shorelines and no catfish present had the highest kōura catches, while lakes like Rotorua and Rotoiti with catfish, eutrophication, stratification, and extensive macrophyte cover yielded substantially lower kōura numbers. Habitat restoration is therefore not a stand-alone solution. Stone pile deployment can address structural habitat availability, but not the other stressors and as a stand-alone action it will have limited effect. Evidence from Chapter 3 suggests that kōura recovery requires water quality improvement, predator management, macrophyte control, and habitat restoration working together, with restoration most likely to succeed in lakes with improving conditions. 

## Fear as a reason to build
Chapter 2 showed that kōura react with heightened refuge use when exposed to catfish compared to native predators like eels. This is consistent with the response expected of a prey species encountering a novel predator, one of which it has no evolutionary history and therefore no calibrated sense of risk [@Sih2023]. This leads to maximum caution, kōura increase their chance of survival, but at a cost. An animal spending more time in refuge is an animal spending less time foraging, leading to slower growth and reduced reproductive outputs. This is the core mechanism behind the landscape of fear [@Laundre2001], where predators do not need to eat every prey individual to suppress a population, but their presence alone is sufficient to reshape how prey use space and time, and those behavioural changes carry demographic consequences. In the context of the Rotorua Te Arawa Lakes, this means that catfish suppress kōura populations through two channels simultaneously. Direct predation removes individuals and fear-induced behavioural change reduces the reproductive capaccity of those that survive. Population recovery under these conditions is likely to be slow even if predation rates are reduced, because the behaviour suppression persists as long as catfish are present. Habitat restoration can play an important intervantion. Chapter 5 showed that stone piles in Lake Rotoiti attracted kōura densities approaching those on natural rocky substrates, despite the ongoing presence of catfish. High-quality refuge does not eliminate the fear response, but it provides kōura with locations where they can shelter effectively, emerge to forage in relative safety, and maintain sufficient activity levels to reproduce. In catfish-invaded lakes where predator eradication is ongoing, habitat provision may therefore be what allows a remnant population to persist and slowly recover rather than declining to local extinction. Stone piles are not solving fear responses but they could be helpful in managing its consequences. 

## Case study - field trial stones piles Lake Ōkaro
Before deploying stone piles in Lake Rotoiti, the construction logistics of loading stones onto a trailer and placing them by hand were first trialled in Lake Ōkaro. Lake Ōkaro is one of the smaller Rotorura Te Arawa volcanic lakes with an area of 30.19 ha, a max depth of 18 m and a mean depth of 12.5 m. Water quality in the lake has been very poor with trophic state flipping between hypereutrophic and eutrophic and frequent algae blooms [@BRON]. To improve water quality in the lake, in 2007 a wetland has been constructed to remove nitrogen and sediments off the main inflows into the lake [@Hudson2009]. Twice a year alum dosings (aluminum sulfate) are executed as in lake phosphorous treatment [@BRON]. Kōura have not been present in Lake Ōkaro in monitorings from 1981 to 2007 [@Parkyn2007] and monitoring in 2016 and 2017 still found no kōura in the lake even as water quality had been improved [@Kusabs2017]. Water quality now should be far enough restored that kōura should be able to survive in the lake but natural recolonisation has not happened yet, therefore reintroduction might be possible. Lake Ōkaro is stocked with trout for angling and as a predator of kōura, extra refuge and habitat by stone piles would be good to trial in this lake. The lake has no stone or rocky substrates therefore the construction of two stone piles would provide initial refuge for the introduced kōura in the lake. 

Construction of the stone piles was executed by all parties involved in the lake with people from Te Arawa Lake Trust, Ngāti Tahu - Ngāti Whaoa, Te Mana o Ngāti Rangitihi Trust, and Bay of Plenty Regional Council. Stones were sourced from Rainbow Quarry located on Rainbow Mountain three km from the lake. Stones used had a size range of 70-210 mm mean 120 mm and a total of 5 m$^{3}$ was divided over two stone piles on the north east shore of the lake. Stone piles were placed 3 and 6 m of the shore, horizontal each 5 m long and 2 meters wide at 1 m depth. To follow dissolved oxygen and temperature development of the stone piles and how they compare to the rest of the lake on 11 September 2024 three HOBO U26-001 Dissolved Oxygen Data Loggers (± 0.2 mg L$^{-1}$ DO accuracy, ± 0.2 °C temperature accuracy; 0–30 mg L$^{-1}$ measurement range; Onset Computer Corporation, Bourne, MA, USA), were placed in the lake for a period of six months. Two loggers were buried inside the stone piles and a third logger was placed outside of the stone piles as control. In October 2024 twenty kōura sourced from within the catchment were reintroduction on the stone piles to start a new population of kōura in the lake. 

In the early summer of 2024 the lake stratified rapidly with oxygen levels dropping quickly and at December 10th oxygen levels reaching less than 3 mg L$^{-1}$ at water surface level for multiple days (@fig-okaro-combined). The HOBO loggers in the stone piles and the control next to the structures showed that inside the structures the dissolved oxygen levels dropped slightly faster than compared to the control site, and that the daily average of dissolved oxygen was lower than outside of the stone piles (@fig-okaro-combined). This is expected as the sensors inside the stone pile received less water mixing and were in close proximity to periphyton, which draws down dissolved oxygen through respiration during the night. During anoxic events, kōura are expected to move to the outer margins of the stone piles or emerge entirely, as DO levels inside the structure would fall below tolerance. Kōura survival through this event is unlikely given the prolonged hypoxic conditions.

![Daily mean water temperature (top) and dissolved oxygen (bottom) at Ōkaro stone pile, control sites, and the lake buoy < 1m. Period: 2024-09-11 to 2025-02-27.](06_synthesis/outputs/fig-okaro-combined.png){#fig-okaro-combined}

Construction of stone piles by hand is a very cheap and easy way of building extra structure and refuge for kōura in the shallows. The reintroduction of kōura was made difficult by a strong anoxic event. Nevertheless the lessons learned were of great value for the further development of the stone pile structure as the stone size in Ōkaro was smaller (range 70-210 mm; mean 120 mm) leading to more effort of getting the stones from the trailer into the lake and smaller stones provide smaller crevice sizes limiting shelter availability for larger size kōura. For deployment in Lake Rotoiti slightly larger stones (85-315 mm; mean 173 mm) were therefore selected to build the stone piles. This size range was very convenient as the size of the stones was requiring machinery for lifting which gave the opportunity to throw the stones further than the end of the trailer and made it possible to rearrange the stones if needed. The Ōkaro trial demonstrated that habitat restoration cannot succeed ahead of water quality recovery. The anoxic event that followed stone pile deployment confirmed that structural complexity provides no benefit when dissolved oxygen drops below kōura tolerance, kōura cannot use a refuge they cannot reach. This reinforces the sequencing argument made later in this chapter: habitat provision must follow, not precede, the establishment of survivable conditions.

## Case study - deep stone piles Lake Rotoiti
All stone piles deployed in the preceding chapters, and in the Ōkaro trial, were built by hand from the shore and therefore limited to shallow, nearshore depths. Extending deployment across a depth gradient required working from a boat, which opened two additional possibilities: placing structures at depths beyond wading distance, and positioning them within invasive macrophyte beds to test whether stone coverage could suppress growth. Monitoring at greater depths is not well suited to standard netting, so spotlighting, remotely operated vehicle (ROV) surveys, and human diving were used before and after construction at this deep Lake Rotoiti site. Spotlighting was executed after sunset along the shoreline and the area around the jetty, by observing for kōura using a torch. Each kōura was counted and their size estimated. The stone piles in the macrophyte bed and deeper were after sunset monitored using a FIFISH V-EVO (4K 60 fps camera; 166° field of view; QYSEA Technology, Shenzhen, China). The ROV was used to swim transects at the location of the stone piles and kōura shown were counted. Kōura CPUE was calculated over the time spent monitoring. Spotlight and ROV monitoring was executed two times before stone pile construction, and day-time diving was executed once before construction.

The construction of four deep stone piles in Lake Rotoiti took place on the 20th of November 2025. Three of the stone piles were constructed perpendicular to the shoreline, with the first one placed underneath a jetty at around 1 m depth. The second was placed in the middle of an invasive macrophyte bed in front of the jetty around 3 m depth, and the third was placed at the end of the macrophytes at a depth from 7 to 9 m. To prevent the stones from sinking into the sandy sediment, wooden slabs were first installed after which the stones were placed, holding the wooden slabs in place. The fourth stone pile was constructed on a bedrock shelf 120 m away from the other stone piles. As these stones were placed on bedrock no wooden slabs were needed. Construction was executed by a boat with a winch lowering bags of stones to the designated location and emptied by divers. 

The first pre monitoring (30/06/2025; water temp 13 °C) resulted in two kōura sightings by spotlighting and two by the ROV, second pre monitoring (26/08/2025; water temp 11 °C) showed zero kōura by spotlighting but seven with the ROV. The human dive did not find any kōura before construction (10/11/2025; water temp 18.7 °C). The monitoring after construction (1/05/2026; water temp 16 °C) revealed ten kōura by spotlighting around the jetty and eleven kōura on the deeper stone piles in front of the jetty monitored by the ROV. Most kōura were seen at the border of the stone piles where they met with the sandy substrate; this was expected as visual it was hard to look inside the stone piles and kōura were observed when emerged and started foraging. The stone pile in the macrophyte bed showed a complete block of macrophyte growth but at the sides the macrophytes were present densely making kōura observations difficult. The human diving did not find any kōura (15/01/2026; water temp 21.7 °C) but noticed many bullies around the stone piles. The second human dive (28/07/2026; water temp ... °C) found ... kōura. 

As kōura are nocturnal daytime monitoring is expected to receive lower kōura counts as they are assumably hidden away between the stones and its not expected that the kōura will be observed outside of their shelter during the day. Footage of the ROV showed that despite the noise of the engines the kōura do emerge from their hiding place and they do not look affected by the monitoring of the ROV. Extending deployment across a depth gradient adds an important dimension absent from the shallow shoreline work of chapter 5. Kōura can access and use refuges below the macrophyte zone, and structures distributed across multiple depths give the flexibility to shift between refuges as thermal stratification seasonally compresses the oxygenated zone upward. Stone piles at depth therefore address not only structural habitat availability but also the habitat compression dynamic identified as a key stressor in the Rotorua Te Arawa Lakes, and the observed suppression of macrophytes at deeper piles suggests an additional co-benefit of restoring structural habitat in overgrown littoral zones and possibly creates corridors connecting deep and shallow habitats. 

## Kōura monitoring approaches
The choice of monitoring method depends on what question is being asked. Methods that sample a general area answer "how many kōura are present in this location?" while active, site specific methods answer "are kōura using this structure?". These are distinct questions, and selecting the right monitoring method for each is relevant. Traditional Māori harvesting methods using tau kōura with whakaweku (bundles of bracken fern *Pteridium esculentum*) have proven effective for kōura monitoring in lakes and stream habitats [@Kusabs2009; @Kusabs2018]. In the shallow wave-exposed zone of a lake, however whakaweku is less suitable as wave action destabilises the bundles, reducing their effectiveness as a consistent shelter substrate. A further limitation in the context of this thesis is that whakaweku creates habitat. Using it to assess whether stone piles attract kōura would confound the monitoring tool with the intervention, making it impossible to determine whether kōura are responding to the stone pile or the whakaweku itself. Passive netting with single-winged fyke nets and baited box traps was therefore used throughout Chapter 3 and 5. Box traps baited with trout flesh in Chapter 3 and catfish flesh in Chapter 5. When comparing catches at Lake Rotoiti sites, no difference was found between bait types. This was expected as kōura scavenge carcases of dead predator fish without bias towards native or non-native species [@MacNeil2025]. Trout as bait yielded a CPUE of 0.682 kōura per trap and catfish bait 0.638 kōura per trap. Unbaited fyke nets had a notably higher CPUE of 2.71 kōura per trap in the same lake, consistent with their larger net opening and longer fishing lead. Fyke nets also catch other fish species, which is useful if community composition is of interest. However, 24-hour passive netting provides an approximation of local abundance rather than direct evidence of habitat use. Trap catches alone cannot determine whether kōura are actively using stone piles or are simply present in the surrounding area. For Chapter 5 higher catches near stone piles were therefore interpreted as indicating greater habitat use of the structures. In structurally complex habitats, fyke nets must be placed flat on the sediment to fish effectively; on rocky substrates this is often not possible, and kōura may pass beneath the net opening entirely. At the deep structures build in Lake Rotoiti, more active monitoring approaches were applied including spotlighting, human dive surveys, and ROV dive surveys. These methods allow direct observation of kōura using the structures and are easier to execute over depth than netting. 

Looking forward, it would be valuable to develop and trial additional monitoring techniques including environmental DNA (eDNA), remote underwater video (RUV), baited remote underwater video (BRUV), and passive acoustic monitoring. Comparative studies suggest that trapping, RUV, BRUV perform similarly for crayfish detection while eDNA has failed to detect crayfish in similar stream system [@Harwood2025; @Harwood2026]. Conversely, eDNA has detected invasive crayfish in lakes where trapping alone could not [@Dougherty2016], suggesting it may be better suited to lake-scale presence-absence assessments than to evaluate kōura use of specific structures. Passive acoustics offer a complementary approach as crayfish produce distinctive sounds during movement and feeding, and acoustic methods have shown potential for identifying crayfish behaviour in field and laboratory settings [@Buscaino2012; @DeVita2023]. Application in marine systems has advanced further, including detection of spiny lobsters over great distances [@Jezequel2020; @Jezequel2023], and the potential for passive acoustic monitoring in lakes warrants exploration for kōura.

## What restoration can and cannot fix
Chapter 3 showed that kōura were most consistently associated with complex rocky habitats and riparian vegetation. Locations where this substrte has been lost or never existed are therefor logical candidates for stone pile restoration. Chapter 4 confirmed that stability and structural complexity are the decisive factors in kōura shelter selection, and stone piles deliver both. In lakes where populations are declining, stone piles can help a remnant population persist and create habitat stepping stones that lower the cost of dispersal between patches, keeping populations more connected while broader lake conditions improve. Stone piles can also physically suppress invasive macrophytes at the placement site as demonstrated in the deep Rotoiti case study. Whether habitat restoration achieves those benefits depends on whether habitat is actually the limiting factor. In a Norwegian lake where muddy substrates limited noble crayfish (*Astacus astacus*) distribution, adding rocky substrate to previously unproductive areas resulted in colonisation and improved harvest [@Johnsen2008]. In that system, habitat was the constraint, and removing it produced a measurable response. Similarly, structure additions to streams have been shown to improve success of crayfish restocking efforst [@Huolila1997]. In both cases, the stressor being addressed was the primary one operating on the population. The situation in the Rotorua Te Arawa Lakes is more complex. Lack of suitable habitat is one of the compounding stressors, and addressing it alone will not produce the same clear response. If dissolved oxygen drops below kōura tolerance, structure is irrelevant as demonstrated by the anoxic event in Lake Ōkaro. If catfish pressure remains high, stone piles improve survival odds but cannot prevent the ongoing suppression from predation and fear effects. Restoration therefore sets a ceiling defined by the other stressors: Chapter 3 showed that the lakes with the best habitat and the best water quality, most complex habitat, and no catfish support the highest kōura abundance. The question is not whether to restore habitat, but at what point in a lake's recovery trajectory habitat becomes the limiting factor, and before that point, improving water quality and reducing predator pressure will produce greater return. The sequence of kōura population recovery would start with stabilising water quality to a point where it falls within kōura acceptable limits, then focus would need to be on predator management, lowering the predation pressure and then habitat recovery and if needed reintroduction.

## Future direction
What have we learned for the future...

### Scaling restoration
When water quality and other aspacts are solved, stonepile deployment in catfish invaded lakes helps

### Community contributions 
Residents living along the lakes could help also instead of having a retaining wall not providing habitat replace it with stones and bring the stones into the deeper parts if possible to create kōura shelters. under jettys placing stones are out of the way of boating or swimming and create perfect structure for kōura. 

![Kōura weels by Mark Robinson Lake Tarawera](images/koura_wheel2.png){#fig-koura-wheel width=70%}

![Kōura pipes by Mark Thomson Lake Rotoiti](images/koura_pipes.png){#fig-koura-pipes width=70%}

To 


### Te Arawa and cultural dimension
Increasing shelter availability in the lake will atrrect kōura which in turn can lead to kōura population recovery with in the future possible recovery of cultural practices as now little is happening in Cherry Bay (Ōkahuroroa Bay) Rotoiti with abundunce tau kōura monitoring brining a high density of whakaweku creating complexity.
